headline: The Original Montana Club Established in Helena, 1885
body:
On April 13, 1885, in the bustling, ambitious heart of Helena, Montana Territory, a curious institution took root, known as The Original Montana Club. Now, you might imagine a club of gentlemen in a place like Montana, back in those days, to be a rough-and-tumble affair, full of prospectors with gold dust in their whiskers and cattlemen smelling faintly of the range. And you wouldn't be entirely wrong about the general populace. But this club, my friends, was a different breed of cat altogether. It was founded by fifty men, a veritable who's who of the territory's burgeoning elite: attorneys with sharp minds, bankers with even sharper ledgers, mining magnates whose fortunes glittered like the very ore they extracted, livestock barons, timber titans, politicians, and those self-described capitalists who seemed to have a finger in every pie, from transportation to wholesale. They gathered not for a game of poker in a smoky saloon, but for "literary, mutual improvement, and social purposes." A grand phrase, that, for what amounted to a gathering of the powerful to discuss, no doubt, the finer points of making more money and influencing more decisions.

By 1891, the membership had swelled, more than quintupling, a clear sign that the thirst for exclusive camaraderie and influence was as potent as any gold strike. The original quarters, however grand they might have seemed at first, proved insufficient for such a burgeoning collection of territorial luminaries. So, they set their sights on a triangular lot just off Helena's bustling North Main Street, a prime piece of real estate for a club that aimed to be the very crown jewel of the capital. Within two years, a six-story edifice of granite and brick rose from the earth, designed by the German-trained architect John Paulsen and his partner, John Lavalle, an MIT man. It opened its doors in 1893, a Gothic Revival marvel with interior appointments that would make a London clubman nod in approval, albeit with an "oriental" flair courtesy of C.O. Rice and Company of St. Paul. It was a place of strict physical and social separations, a veritable fortress of male privilege, offering its members a private retreat for leisurely conversation, gambling, and dining, a home away from home for the men who shaped Montana.

But fate, as it often does, had a cruel jest in store for this monument to territorial ambition. On a snowy April evening in 1903, a fire, suspected to be the work of an arsonist, swept through the grand building, leaving it a gutted shell. The smoke had barely cleared before the resolve of these Montana titans hardened. A building committee, led by attorney E.C. Day and the Helena Independent's owner, John S.M. Neill, wasted no time. They telegraphed Cass Gilbert, a St. Paul-based architect of considerable renown, who had cut his teeth with the New York firm of McKim, Mead and White. Gilbert, though busy with the Minnesota State Capitol, took on the commission, sending preliminary drawings within six weeks. The club's Board of Governors authorized a plan not to exceed $105,000, a princely sum in those days, for a new structure.

Gilbert, with a nod to Sienese architecture, conjured a new, wedge-shaped building from the ashes, rising from the triangular lot with rusticated granite and local paving brick from Helena's Western Clay Manufacturing Company. It was a six-story marvel, complete with a broad projecting cornice and a painted wood frieze ornamented with shields. The main entrance, on the sloping south side, featured a roughly-chiseled granite archway soaring above a swastika encaustic tile floor, a detail that would, in later years, carry a far different and darker connotation. Inside, the new club was a symphony of luxury and purpose: conversation rooms, a loggia overlooking the Reading Room, Billiard Room, and Main Hall, card rooms, and bedrooms for members. The sixth floor boasted a main dining room, an English hall capable of seating 75, illuminated by stained glass windows depicting vignettes of Native Americans, tipis, log homes, a gold miner, and a stagecoach, all illustrating "the progress of civilization in Montana." In the basement, the "Rathskeller" was a cozy retreat, lined with "Leatharole" and dark fir woodwork, recalling similar designs Gilbert had crafted for the Minnesota State Capitol. When this magnificent structure opened in the early summer of 1905, the Helena Daily Record, with a flourish of local pride, proclaimed it "[a] Dream in Architecture…a magnificent structure complete in every detail of furnishings and equipment from Rathskeller to ‘sky floor.’" It was, indeed, a powerful reaffirmation of Helena's cosmopolitan stature and its enduring role as the hub of Montana's political and economic power, a testament to the resilience and ambition of its founders, who, like the very mountains surrounding their city, refused to be easily moved.
